title: Extremes (MAX and MIN)
description: Find the highest and lowest values instantly.

slides.title: Finding the Biggest 🏆
slides.content:
# The MAX function

Want to know the most expensive product in your store? Without aggregation, you'd have to `ORDER BY price DESC LIMIT 1`. That works... but there's a better, more mathematical way.

Use **`MAX()`**! 

`SELECT MAX(price) FROM products;`
This instantly scans the entire table and returns the single highest number.

quizData.question: If you want to find the highest score a player ever achieved in a `game_history` table, what function do you use?
quizData.explanation: MAX() is the standard SQL aggregation function for finding the single maximum value in a column.

slides.title: Finding the Smallest 🐭
slides.content:
## The MIN function

Conversely, if you want the absolutely lowest/smallest value (e.g., the cheapest product), you use **`MIN()`**.

`SELECT MIN(price) FROM products;`

slides.title: Dates and Text 📅
slides.content:
## It's not just for numbers!

`MIN()` and `MAX()` work brilliantly on Dates and Alphabetical Text, too!

* `MIN(date_of_birth)`: Gives you the oldest person (earliest date)
* `MAX(date_of_birth)`: Gives you the youngest person (latest date)
* `MAX(last_name)`: Gives you the name closest to the letter Z.

quizData.question: If you run `SELECT MIN(join_date) FROM employees;`, what will it return?

options.text: The date of the most recently hired employee
The date of the FIRST/OLDEST hired employee in the entire company
An error, it only works on numbers

quizData.explanation: For dates, MIN() returns the earliest point in time, and MAX() returns the most recent point in time.

slides.title: Try it Out!
slides.content: Find the lowest `price` in the products table and alias it `cheapest_item`.
slides.codeSnippet:
SELECT MIN(price) AS cheapest_item
FROM products;
